title: Dyeing with Japanese indigo
subtitle: A fresh-leaf cold extraction method for dyeing wool and silk a medium blue-green without a fermentation vat
excerpt: Japanese indigo (Persicaria tinctoria) can be processed as a fresh-leaf extraction without a fermentation vat. Blending fresh leaves with slightly acidic water releases the indigo precursor, which then oxidises directly onto dampened protein fibre. The process is time-sensitive and must be completed within minutes of blending.
sourceNotes: Fresh-leaf Japanese indigo extraction is documented by Sasha Duerr in 'The Handbook of Natural Plant Dyes' and in UK natural dye guild teaching. The process chemistry (enzymatic release of indican) is consistent across sources. Observation-led from guild instruction.

glossaryTerms.definition: A cold process for Japanese indigo dyeing in which fresh leaves are blended with slightly acidic water to release the indigo precursor. The extract is strained and immediately applied to dampened fibre; the whole process from blending to rinsing must be completed within 10 to 15 minutes before the indigo oxidises out of solution.

The colourless, water-soluble precursor compound in fresh indigo leaves. When leaves are crushed or blended in acidic water, plant enzymes convert indican to indigo. The indigo immediately begins oxidising (turning blue) on contact with air; applying the extract to fibre before this oxidation is complete is the key timing challenge in fresh-leaf extraction.

The conventional method for indigo dyeing in which insoluble indigo powder is chemically reduced to a soluble form in an alkaline, oxygen-depleted liquid (the vat). Fibre dipped in the vat absorbs soluble indigo; on removal, the indigo oxidises back to insoluble blue on the fibre surface. Fresh-leaf extraction bypasses this process entirely.

. This cold process does not require a fermentation
setup of conventional indigo work. The colour comes from

released from the blended leaves. The timing is tight. Have everything ready before blending begins. Japanese indigo grows readily as a half-hardy annual. The Garden category covers raising it from seed and harvesting schedules for successive leaf crops.

attrs.title: Equipment safety
attrs.body: Designate utensils used for natural dyeing separately from food preparation. Never reuse dye equipment for food.

items.name: Fresh Japanese indigo leaves (Persicaria tinctoria), stripped from stems
Wool or silk, pre-wetted in water, dry weight
White wine vinegar or citric acid, to acidify the water
Blender or food processor, designated for dyeing only
Fine mesh strainer or muslin cloth for straining leaf pulp
Large bowl or pot for the extraction

content.text: Pre-wet the fibre in cool water for at least 30 minutes before dyeing. Have the straining equipment and the fibre ready to go before you blend the leaves: once the blending begins, you have 10 to 15 minutes before the indigo oxidises out of solution. Chill the water beforehand if possible; cold water slows oxidation slightly and extends the working window.

Add the vinegar or citric acid to the cold water and confirm it tastes slightly sour: the water should be mildly acidic. Add the fresh leaves to the blender with the acidified water and blend for 30 seconds until thoroughly liquefied. Strain immediately through muslin or a fine strainer into a wide bowl, pressing the leaf pulp to extract all liquid. The liquid will be greenish-yellow at this stage. Submerge the pre-wetted fibre fully in the extract and squeeze it gently through the liquid for 5 to 8 minutes. Remove the fibre and expose it to air: the colour on the fibre will shift from yellow-green to blue as the indigo oxidises. Rinse in cool water and hang to dry away from direct sunlight.

items.problem: The fibre is yellow-green after rinsing and does not shift to blue
items.cause: The indican oxidised to insoluble indigo before the fibre absorbed it. This happens when the extraction runs past the 15-minute window.
items.fix: The process must run faster: blend and strain in under 5 minutes total, then submerge the fibre immediately. If the extract liquid is already turning blue-grey before the fibre goes in, the usable window has passed.

items.problem: The colour is very faint and uneven
items.cause: The leaf quantity was insufficient, or the fibre was not fully submerged throughout
items.fix: Use a minimum of 4:1 leaf weight to fibre weight (400 g leaves per 100 g fibre). Use a container narrow enough that the fibre stays submerged without rising above the liquid surface.
